text: Recent advances in spatial omics have expanded the spectrum of profiled molecular categories beyond transcriptomics. However, many of these technologies are constrained by limited spatial resolution, hindering our ability to deeply characterize intricate tissue architectures. Existing computational methods primarily focus on the resolution enhancement of transcriptomics data, lacking the adaptability to address the emerging spatial omics technologies that profile various omics types. Here, we introduce soScope, a unified generative framework designed to enhance data quality and spatial resolution for molecular profiles obtained from diverse spatial technologies. soScope aggregates multimodal tissue information from omics, spatial relations and images, and jointly infers omics profiles at enhanced resolutions with omics-specific modeling through distribution priors. With comprehensive evaluations on diverse spatial omics platforms, including Visium, Xenium, spatial-CUT&Tag, and slide-DNA/RNA-seq, soScope improves performances in identifying biologically meaningful intestine and kidney architectures, revealing embryonic heart structure that cannot be resolved at the original resolution and correcting sample and technical biases arising from sequencing and sample processing. Furthermore, soScope extends to spatial multiomics technology spatial-CITE-seq and spatial ATAC-RNA-seq, leveraging cross-omics reference for simultaneous multiomics enhancement. soScope provides a versatile tool to improve the utilization of continually expanding spatial omics technologies and resources.

Tissues are built upon cells with distinct molecular states and spatial organizations. Extensive dissection of tissue architecture requires the deep characterization of diverse molecular profiles while preserving their spatial contexts. In recent years, significant advances in spatial omics technologies have enabled the spatial profiling of various molecular categories, including transcript (e.g., ST, Visium, Xenium, Stereo-seq, seqFISH, slide-seq, STARmap, and MERFISH), protein (e.g., DBiT-seq and spatial-CITE-seq), epigenetic marker (e.g., spatial ATAC-seq, spatial ATAC-RNA-seq, and spatial-CUT&Tag), and genomic variation (e.g., slide-DNA-seq and DNA-seqFISH). By unveiling the spatial signatures of diverse molecular profiles, spatial omics offers valuable insights across various biological areas, such as cancer, Alzheimer's disease, and embryo development. Despite the early success of existing spatial omics approaches, two challenges remain limiting their full potential. Firstly, tissues are often in frozen or formalin-fixed before sequencing. The freezing process and formalin-induced deamination can potentially impact the molecule states, leading to reduced sequencing accuracy. Secondly, the majority of spatial technologies use spatial barcodes to mark the positions of targeted molecules and profile omics at the tissue spot resolution. As each spot typically contains tens of cells, the multi-cell averaged sequencing readouts reduce the heterogeneity that can be resolved, leading to limited spatial resolution in tissue structure. Computational techniques hold the promise to reduce data variations and enhance spatial resolutions in spatial omics data, facilitating a more precise characterization of tissue architectures. However, current methods predominantly enhance omics profiles from a single tissue modality, such as spatial positions (e.g., BayesSpace) or image (e.g., XFuse and iStar), which overlook the rich, multimodal tissue information available from most spatial platforms. Furthermore, these approaches are primarily tailored for transcriptomics data, where their statistical assumptions are not extendable to other spatial omics categories. Here, we introduce spatial omics scope (soScope), a fully generative framework that models the generation process of spot-level profiles from diverse spatial omics technologies and aims to enhance their spatial resolution and data quality. To achieve this, soScope views each spot as the aggregation of “subspots” at an enhanced spatial resolution, with their omics profiles associated with spatial positions and morphological patterns. Then, soScope integrates spot omics profiles, spatial relations, and high-resolution morphological images using a multimodal deep learning framework and jointly infers omics profiles at the subspot resolution. By selecting omics-specific distributions, soScope allows for accurate modeling and variation reduction of different spatial omics data. soScope offers a unified tool that incorporates multimodal tissue profiles to enhance omics profiles with different molecular categories. With comprehensive benchmarks on diverse spatial omics types (transcripts, epigenetics, DNA, and proteins) from multiple biological systems, we demonstrate that soScope can effectively enhance spatial resolutions, reduce unwanted variations, and enable the characterization of complex tissue structure that cannot be detected at the original resolution. Furthermore, soScope extends to the emerging spatial multiomics technology, integrates multimodal, multiomics tissue profiles, and simultaneously enhances their resolutions.

soScope is a unified generative model that integrates molecular profiles (X), spatial neighboring relations (A), and morphological image features (Y) from diverse spatial omics platforms and aims to divide each spot into multiple subspots (X'). The model includes three steps: (1) spot-level representation learning: molecular profiles and their spatial coordinates at the original resolution are embedded into latent representations (Z) with a graph encoder q(Z|X,A); (2) subspot-level image feature learning: image patches corresponding to subspot regions are segmented from high-resolution tissue images and transformed into subspots' morphological features; (3) subspot-level omics profile inference: spot representations and subspot image features are combined in an enhancement decoder p(X'|Y,Z) to jointly infer subspot-level profiles that are constrained by the omics-specific distribution and morphological similarities. The graph encoder provides high-quality embeddings for spot-level omics profiles with the preservation of spatial neighboring relations. The image features are extracted from exact subspot regions and provide information at an enhanced resolution to guide the decoder. The decoder enables the accurate modeling of diverse subspot omics data with a probabilistic distribution reflecting its statistic variations. By optimizing its evidence lower bound (ELBO), soScope efficiently generates omics profiles with reduced data noise and enhanced spatial resolutions for a finer characterization of tissue structures. The log-likelihood form for the resolution enhancement process is given by: $\log p(X\mid Y)=\log\left\{\sum_{\widehat{X}}\sum_{Z} p(X\mid\widehat{X}) p(\widehat{X}\mid Y, Z) p(Z)\right\}$. We formulate the overall optimization function by combining ELBO and image regularization: $L = \text{ELBO} - \beta L_{\text{Image}}$, where $\beta$ is a hyperparameter balancing the level of constraint.

We initially evaluated soScope on spatial transcriptomics (ST) datasets generated from diverse platforms. We compared it with three established approaches: iStar and XFuse, which utilize image information to enhance transcript profiles, and BayesSpace, which employs spatial coordinates to infer high-resolution expression data. We assessed a human intestine dataset generated using the Visium platform, containing 2,649 sequencing spots. We examined three functional regions: epithelium, muscularis, and immune regions. soScope achieved reasonable performance in all three regions. From quantitative assessments using Pearson correlation and mean square error (MSE), soScope consistently demonstrated the highest consistency and the lowest reconstruction error. We also evaluated a mouse head dataset obtained from the Xenium platform, covering 1538 cells. We divided the tissue into small square regions and aggregated the cells' gene expressions within each region, aiming to recover their cellular expressions. In the analysis, we used the top 5 abundant and the top 5 marker genes. soScope demonstrated a more consistent expression pattern with the ground truth compared to iStar, which tended to overestimate expression levels in regions of high cell density. Lastly, we investigated how the image input could affect soScope's performance by replacing the image with standard Gaussian noise. For a human ductal carcinoma dataset profiled by Xenium, results showed that soScope without image input did not reach the accuracy of standard soScope on highly correlated genes, while for lowly correlated genes, no significant difference was observed.

section: Results - Application to Spatial-CUT&Tag and Multi-scale Test
text: We applied soScope to a spatial chromatin accessibility dataset generated by spatial-CUT&Tag from an embryonic day 11 mouse embryo. We selected the top 60 variable peaks from four major organ regions (liver, heart, forebrain, and spinal cord). We compared soScope with six general machine learning models: spatial-based (Spatial linear, Spatial GP) and image-based (Image linear, Image GP, Image MLP) approaches. In a simulation study merging neighboring spots, soScope achieved the highest Pearson correlation and lowest MSE in recovering peak counts at the original resolution. When enhancing the tissue resolution by 4 folds, soScope correctly identified expressed regions while effectively suppressing out-of-region expressions, unlike other methods that were confused by random noise. To test robustness, we designed a multi-scale stress test using a mouse liver tumor dataset profiled by slide-DNA/RNA-seq. We merged neighboring regions at different sizes (from 2x2 to 10x10) to generate low-resolution profiles. soScope consistently showed stable performance at different resolutions, with a relatively small decrease in accuracy. Quantitative evaluation using Pearson correlation and MSE confirmed that soScope maintained high accuracies in preserving gene expression patterns for regional marker genes across various resolutions, outperforming other methods which showed expected decreased accuracies as resolution decreased.

Recent advancements in spatial multiomics have expanded our capabilities for spatial tissue profiling beyond single omics types. We enabled soScope to work with spatial multiomics (termed multi-soScope) using modifications: (1) The spatial graph is constructed based on similarities from both transcript and protein, providing a combined measurement of spatial heterogeneity; (2) Features from both transcript and protein modalities are input to the encoder to generate joint latent representations; (3) The decoder outputs distribution parameters for enhanced multiomics profiles simultaneously. We applied multi-soScope to a spatial-CITE-seq dataset from human skin tissue post-COVID-19 vaccination. The dataset consisted of 1,618 spots with 15,486 genes and 283 proteins. Original transcript clusters exhibited high noise, whereas protein data showed stronger spatial colocalization. multi-soScope leveraged protein data to contribute to the generation of high-resolution transcript profiles. Enhanced gene expressions revealed tissue structures with improved spatial coherence, and correlations between transcripts and proteins significantly improved after enhancement. Similarly, for a spatial ATAC-RNA-seq dataset from a mouse embryo (E13), multi-soScope improved spatial enrichment of gene expressions and suppressed sporadic expressions within the tissue, further validating its utility in integrating and enhancing multiomic data.

Developing spatial omics technologies have enabled the spatial profiling of various molecular categories, but challenges remain in data quality and resolution. We propose soScope, a generative framework that models the data generation process of spatial omics profiles. By combining omics profiles, spatial neighboring relations, and morphological images, soScope infers omics profiles at an enhanced resolution with omics-specific modeling for data variations. We extensively evaluated soScope on multiple molecule types and platforms, showing it refines tissue domain identifications and corrects technical bias. A limitation is that while soScope provides enhanced profiles for predefined subspots, it may not reach true subcellular resolution without paired single-cell data. Future work could incorporate human labels in a semi-supervised manner or apply the model to larger datasets to reduce computational costs. The soScope framework models the generation process as: $x^{(n)}=\sum_{k=1}^{K}\widehat{x}_{k}^{(n)}+e^{(n)}$, where $\widehat{x}_{k}^{(n)}$ are latent subspot profiles and $e^{(n)}$ is Gaussian noise. The variational inference optimizes the ELBO: $ELBO=E_{q(Z\mid X, A)}\left\{E_{p(\widehat{X}\mid Y, Z)}\left[\log p(X\mid\widehat{X})\right]\right\}-D_{KL}[q(Z\mid X, A)\| p(Z)]$. The model is optimized using the Adam optimizer, and the final enhanced profiles are obtained from the expectation of the posterior distribution.
